nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers, streams, lakes and ponds with soft sand or silt substrates; tolerant of brackish water in coastal estuaries.

decorPreferences: soft sand or fine gravel substrate (burrowable)
driftwood and rockwork for biofilm grazing

diet.notes: Generally documented feeding on detritus, biofilm, soft algae and decaying plant matter; will also take leftover fish/shrimp food. Supplemental feeding is rarely needed in an established tank and tends to fuel population booms.

summary: The Malaysian Trumpet Snail is a live-bearing, nocturnal burrower widely kept for its role in aerating sand substrates and preventing the build-up of harmful anaerobic gas pockets in planted aquariums.
careNotes: Malaysian Trumpet Snails are among the more polarising inhabitants in the freshwater hobby — actively sought by some keepers for their substrate work, and a persistent nuisance for others who find them difficult to control once established. They spend the day burrowed in sand or fine gravel, emerging at night to graze biofilm and detritus, and their constant tunnelling is generally credited with preventing anaerobic gas pockets and helping mix mulm into the substrate for plant roots. They tend to tolerate temperatures from roughly 21 to 28 °C and pH 7.0 to 8.0, with moderate to hard water preferred for shell development. Populations are parthenogenetic and live-bearing — a single snail can clone itself into a large colony — so population explosions are almost always a symptom of overfeeding rather than the snails themselves; the standard control is to feed less rather than to add chemicals. Like all freshwater invertebrates, they are highly sensitive to copper-based medications and should be removed from a tank before any copper treatment is applied. A burrowable substrate (sand or fine gravel, at least 2.5 cm deep) is the one non-negotiable requirement; without it they cannot perform their characteristic substrate-stirring role and tend to crawl the glass instead. They are not known to climb out of tanks. Assassin Snails, loaches, and pufferfish will readily prey on them — a useful consideration depending on whether the goal is to grow or cull a population.
breedingNotes: Malaysian Trumpet Snails give birth to live young — rather than laying eggs, females carry developing snails internally and release them as miniature versions of the adult. Reproduction is largely parthenogenetic, meaning females can produce clonal young without needing a mate, so a single snail introduced as a hitchhiker on a plant or decoration is enough to seed an entire colony. Sexual reproduction also occurs where males are present. Maturity is generally reached at three to five months, and a mature female can release several dozen young at a time. Population control is most effectively achieved by limiting food input rather than by attempting to remove individuals.
